q: You need to build a pipeline that processes genomics VCF files stored in Cloud Storage. Processing requires complex bioinformatics tools (bwa, samtools) not available in standard Dataflow transforms. What is the best approach?
opts: Rewrite the bioinformatics tools in pure Python using Biopython and PySAM and run them inside a Dataflow ParDo with native package imports per worker

Use Dataflow with custom Docker containers (Flex Templates) containing the bioinformatics tools; or use Life Sciences API (Cloud Batch) which natively supports custom container-based genomic workflows
Use Dataproc with a custom initialization action that installs bwa and samtools on each cluster node and runs the bioinformatics pipeline as a Spark job
Install the bioinformatics tools on Dataflow workers using pip in the pipeline setup file, layering the binaries on top of the default Beam worker image
explain: For specialized scientific tools, Dataflow Flex Templates with custom Docker containers (FROM apache/beam_python3.10_sdk; RUN apt-get install bwa samtools) allows arbitrary tool execution. Alternatively, Cloud Batch (formerly Life Sciences API) is purpose-built for container-based scientific batch workloads.
concept: Dataflow custom containers, Flex Templates, scientific computing, Cloud Batch

q: What is the significance of the 'committed' vs 'buffered' write modes in the BigQuery Storage Write API?
opts: Buffered mode is intended for batch Dataflow jobs while committed mode is restricted to streaming pipelines only, with neither providing exactly-once guarantees

Committed mode provides exactly-once guarantees with atomic row-level commits; buffered mode provides lower latency with rows visible within seconds but possible duplicates on failure. Default stream (legacy) provides no ordering guarantee
Both buffered and committed modes provide identical write guarantees with the same exactly-once semantics; buffered is simply the newer rebranded API mode
Committed mode is only available in BigQuery Enterprise Edition reservations and not in Standard or on-demand mode, due to its higher slot consumption per write
explain: Storage Write API stream types: Default stream (legacy, at-least-once, rows visible immediately). Committed mode: rows are appended to committed streams with offset-based exactly-once guarantee; rows visible on commit. Buffered mode: rows held in a buffer, committed in batches — balances latency and exactly-once-ness.
concept: BigQuery Storage Write API, committed vs buffered streams, exactly-once guarantees

q: A company's data team has 5 data engineers, 10 data analysts, and 3 data scientists, all needing different levels of BigQuery access. What is the recommended IAM structure?
opts: Grant each individual person a custom IAM role with exactly the permissions they need, configured individually per user via gcloud iam policies for fine-grained control
Create three Google Groups (data-engineers, data-analysts, data-scientists); assign BigQuery IAM roles to groups at appropriate levels (project/dataset/table); manage individual membership in groups
Assign roles/bigquery.admin to all users across the data team for simplicity and rely on BigQuery audit logs to retroactively detect any inappropriate access patterns
Create a shared service account that all team members impersonate via roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator, centralizing access control on the service account bindings
explain: Google recommends managing BigQuery access via Google Groups. Groups provide scalable, auditable access management. Typical bindings: data-engineers → roles/bigquery.dataEditor at project level; data-analysts → roles/bigquery.dataViewer on specific datasets; data-scientists → roles/bigquery.jobUser + viewer on ML datasets.
concept: BigQuery IAM best practices, Google Groups, role-based access

q: You need to monitor data quality in a production BigQuery pipeline and alert when more than 5% of records fail validation rules. Which approach automates this?
opts: Manually sample the BigQuery table daily and count invalid records using a spreadsheet, then notify the team if the failure rate exceeds 5% of total records sampled

Use Dataplex Data Quality rules with expectation metrics (PERCENT_NULL, REGEX_MATCH, etc.); configure the quality scan to write results to BigQuery; create a Cloud Monitoring alert on the Dataplex data quality metric when failure rate exceeds 5%
Write a Cloud Function triggered by Cloud Scheduler that runs a BigQuery validation query nightly and sends an email if the failure rate exceeds the 5% threshold
Add a Dataflow ParDo that counts invalid records inline and writes counts to Stackdriver (Cloud Logging), then build a log-based metric and alert on its threshold
explain: Dataplex Data Quality scans define rules (not_null, regex_match, value_in_set, custom SQL) and execute on a schedule. Results (including pass/fail percentages) are logged to Cloud Monitoring. An alerting policy on the dataplex.googleapis.com/data_quality/... metric fires when failure rate > 5%.
concept: Dataplex Data Quality, automated data quality monitoring, Cloud Monitoring alerts

q: Which Google Cloud product allows you to create a complete Apache Iceberg table format on top of Cloud Storage, accessible from BigQuery, Dataproc, and Spark?
opts: BigQuery managed tables with Iceberg compatibility mode that store data internally in BigQuery's columnar format with an external Iceberg metadata layer for reads
BigLake Managed Tables (Iceberg on BigLake) — data stored in Cloud Storage as Iceberg format; accessible from BigQuery, Dataproc Spark, and any Iceberg-compatible engine
Dataproc Metastore configured with Iceberg catalog support only, exposing Iceberg tables to Spark and Flink but not directly queryable from BigQuery without export
Cloud Spanner with Iceberg export enabled, which materializes Spanner tables into Cloud Storage as Iceberg format for downstream BigQuery and Dataproc queries
explain: BigLake Managed Tables implement the Apache Iceberg open table format on Cloud Storage. They support ACID transactions, time travel, schema evolution, and partition evolution. Both BigQuery (via BigLake external tables) and Dataproc Spark/Flink access the same Iceberg table with full ACID guarantees.
concept: BigLake Managed Tables, Apache Iceberg, open table format, multi-engine access

q: A data engineering team needs to document their BigQuery data assets including business definitions, ownership, and SLAs. Which GCP service is purpose-built for this?
opts: Use BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES with custom user-defined columns appended via labels for storing business definitions, ownership, and SLA documentation
Dataplex Universal Catalog — supports business glossaries, data owner tags, custom metadata aspects, and SLA documentation alongside technical metadata
Use Cloud Monitoring dashboards with custom labels per table to document business definitions, ownership metadata, and SLA expectations alongside metric data
Use a Google Sheets spreadsheet shared with the team as the source of truth for table definitions, owners, and SLAs, linked from each BigQuery table description
explain: Dataplex Universal Catalog (evolved from Data Catalog) enables attaching custom metadata aspects and tag templates to BigQuery tables: business owner, data classification, SLA, quality score, glossary terms. This creates a unified business + technical metadata layer discoverable across the organization.
concept: Dataplex Universal Catalog, data documentation, metadata management, business glossary

q: A Pub/Sub topic receives messages from multiple publishers. Some messages have duplicate message IDs. What behavior should a correctly designed consumer expect?
opts: Pub/Sub guarantees exactly-once delivery and automatically deduplicates messages with the same publisher-assigned message ID across all subscriptions on the topic
Pub/Sub guarantees at-least-once delivery — duplicates are possible even without publisher duplicates. Consumers must implement idempotent processing or deduplication using message IDs
Pub/Sub Lite guarantees exactly-once delivery semantics while standard Pub/Sub cannot handle duplicate message IDs and silently drops any second copy on receipt
Duplicate message IDs cause Pub/Sub to automatically reject the second message at publish time, returning an ALREADY_EXISTS error to the publisher application
explain: Standard Pub/Sub guarantees at-least-once delivery. Even with unique publisher message IDs, network retries can cause the same message to be delivered twice. Consumer applications must be idempotent (processing the same message twice produces the same result) or perform deduplication using insertion tables keyed on message ID.
concept: Pub/Sub delivery guarantees, at-least-once, consumer idempotency, deduplication

q: You have a Cloud Composer environment running critical pipelines. The Airflow webserver becomes slow during peak DAG execution. What should you do?
opts: Restart the Airflow webserver pod via kubectl to clear accumulated memory, since webserver slowness during peak execution is typically due to memory pressure
Upgrade the Composer environment to a larger environment size (scale up the webserver and scheduler resources); optimize DAG count and complexity to reduce scheduler load
Switch from Cloud Composer to self-managed Apache Airflow on GKE for manual scaling control of the webserver, scheduler, and worker components per workload
Disable the Airflow webserver entirely and rely on gcloud composer environments run commands for DAG triggering and inspection, removing webserver load entirely
explain: Webserver slowness during peak execution is usually due to resource contention. In Composer 2, you can independently scale webserver resources. Also: reduce DAG complexity (fewer Python imports, simpler task graph), use concurrency limits, and ensure scheduler resources are adequate for the DAG count.
concept: Cloud Composer sizing, webserver scaling, Composer 2 resource configuration

q: A Dataflow pipeline reads from BigQuery and the read is taking longer than expected. The BigQuery table has 100 partitions but Dataflow is using only 10 read streams. How do you increase read parallelism?
opts: Increase the Dataflow worker count and rely on autoscaling, which will automatically increase the number of parallel BigQuery read streams in proportion
Use BigQueryIO.read().withTemplateCompatibility() to force the connector to open more read streams against the BigQuery Storage Read API for parallelism
Use BigQueryIO.read().withMethod(DIRECT_READ) which uses the BigQuery Storage Read API with configurable parallelism, or set .withMaxStreamCount()
Read the BigQuery data into Cloud Storage as Avro files first using a BigQuery export job, then read from Cloud Storage with Dataflow for higher parallelism
explain: BigQueryIO has two read modes: EXPORT (copies to GCS then reads — slow) and DIRECT_READ (uses BigQuery Storage Read API for parallel streaming). DIRECT_READ with withMaxStreamCount() controls the number of parallel Storage API streams, directly mapping to read parallelism. This is the fastest way to read large BigQuery tables in Dataflow.
concept: BigQuery Storage Read API, Dataflow DIRECT_READ, parallel read streams

q: You need to implement a Blue/Green deployment for a BigQuery transformation pipeline (dbt project). How would you structure this in GCP?
opts: BigQuery does not support Blue/Green deployment patterns natively; transformation changes must be applied in-place to the production dataset using dbt run --full-refresh

Maintain two BigQuery datasets (blue and prod): run dbt against the blue dataset; validate results; use BigQuery dataset copy or view swapping to atomically promote blue to prod; the switch is instant with no downtime
Create two separate GCP projects, one for blue and one for green environments, with separate BigQuery datasets, and promote by repointing dbt profiles between projects
Use BigQuery's ALTER TABLE RENAME statement to swap production and staging tables atomically, providing instant promotion of the validated transformation results
explain: Blue/Green for BigQuery transformations: dbt runs against a blue dataset (separate from prod). After validation, promote: either rename/swap datasets atomically, or update views in the prod dataset to point to blue table names. This gives zero-downtime promotion with instant rollback capability.
concept: Blue/Green deployment, BigQuery dataset promotion, zero-downtime transformation

q: A streaming pipeline with Dataflow receives 1,000,000 events/sec. The processing logic is CPU-intensive (parsing and transforming JSON). Workers are CPU-bound at 100%. What is the correct scaling action?
opts: Increase the worker boot disk size to speed up I/O-limited shuffle operations during the streaming JSON parse stage, which is typically disk-bound on Dataflow

Add more Dataflow workers (increase maxNumWorkers); if using Streaming Engine, increase n2-standard machine type to higher CPU; Dataflow autoscaling should handle this if maxNumWorkers is configured correctly
Switch from Dataflow to Dataproc Spark Streaming for CPU-intensive workloads, since Spark's executor model handles CPU-bound transformations more efficiently
Reduce the number of concurrent ParDo operations in the pipeline graph by fusing multiple JSON transformations into a single ParDo to lower CPU context-switching
explain: CPU-bound Dataflow workers need either more workers (horizontal scaling via maxNumWorkers) or larger machines (higher CPU count per worker, e.g., n2-highcpu-16). With Streaming Engine, autoscaling adds workers automatically. The root cause is often JSON parsing overhead — consider switching to Avro/Protobuf to reduce parsing cost.
concept: Dataflow CPU scaling, machine types, streaming performance, serialization formats

q: What is the purpose of BigQuery's column-level security using Policy Tags in Dataplex?
opts: Policy tags encrypt column data at rest using a per-tag Cloud KMS key, so any user without access to the key receives an Access Denied response when querying the column

Policy tags define a taxonomy of sensitive data types; columns tagged with a policy tag require the reader to have a specific IAM role (Fine-Grained Reader) to see that column's data — others get Access Denied
Policy tags are documentation metadata only and do not affect BigQuery query execution; users see all column values regardless of tag assignment or IAM grants
Policy tags replace row access policies for row-level filtering by tagging rows rather than columns, restricting visibility based on the row's tag classification
explain: BigQuery column-level security (CLS) uses Dataplex Policy Tag taxonomies. Tag a column (e.g., SSN) with a sensitive tag. Users without roles/datacatalog.categoryFineGrainedReader on that tag get 'Access Denied' when querying that column — even if they have access to the table. This enforces column-level data access control.
concept: BigQuery column-level security, Policy Tags, Dataplex taxonomy, Fine-Grained Reader

q: You need to ingest streaming data from Apache Kafka (on-premises) to BigQuery. Which GCP service provides a managed connector for this without writing custom code?
opts: Cloud Pub/Sub combined with Kafka MirrorMaker 2 for replicating Kafka topics into Pub/Sub topics and then ingesting to BigQuery via a subscription
Dataflow with the Apache Beam Kafka connector (KafkaIO); or use the managed Dataflow Kafka-to-BigQuery template
Cloud Data Fusion configured with the Kafka source plugin and the BigQuery sink plugin, building a graphical pipeline that reads from Kafka into BigQuery
All three GCP options require custom code; there is no fully managed Kafka-to-BigQuery connector available without writing Beam or Spark source code
explain: Dataflow provides a managed Kafka-to-BigQuery template: configure Kafka bootstrap servers, topic, schema, and BigQuery destination — no code needed. Alternatively, KafkaIO in Beam gives code-level control. Both are production-grade. Pub/Sub cannot directly ingest from Kafka without a bridge.
concept: Kafka to BigQuery, Dataflow Kafka connector, KafkaIO, managed templates

q: A data governance policy requires that all production BigQuery tables have a defined owner, data classification, and retention period. How do you enforce this organization-wide?
opts: Document the governance requirements in a wiki page and rely on team compliance through code review processes to ensure all production tables meet the standards

Create mandatory tag templates in Dataplex (owner, classification, retention_days fields); use Organization Policy or Dataplex metadata enforcement to require tags before tables can be queried; automate tagging in CI/CD pipelines
Use BigQuery table descriptions to annotate each table description text with the required fields (owner, classification, retention) in a structured comment format
Apply IAM conditions on each BigQuery table that check for the presence of required tags as policy attributes before granting any read access to the underlying data
explain: Dataplex tag templates define required metadata fields. Create a mandatory template and use Dataplex data quality + governance automation: CI/CD pipeline (Cloud Build + Terraform) applies tags at table creation time. Dataplex policies can enforce tag completeness. This shifts governance left — metadata is enforced at creation, not retroactively.
concept: Dataplex tag templates, metadata enforcement, governance automation, CI/CD data governance

q: A pipeline fails because a Dataflow job cannot read from a Cloud Storage bucket in a different GCP project. All IAM permissions are correct. What else might be causing the issue?
opts: Cross-project Cloud Storage access from Dataflow is not supported by the BigQuery Storage Read API and requires copying the source bucket into the Dataflow project
The Cloud Storage bucket is in a VPC Service Controls perimeter that does not include the Dataflow job's project. Add the Dataflow project to the perimeter or create an access level exception
Dataflow workers must be deployed in the same region as the Cloud Storage bucket; cross-region reads from Cloud Storage are blocked by default in production projects
The Cloud Storage bucket requires Requester Pays to be enabled for cross-project access from Dataflow workers running under a different project's service account
explain: VPC Service Controls perimeters are a common cause of cross-project access failures even when IAM is correct. If the Cloud Storage bucket's project is in a perimeter, all access must come from within the perimeter. Add the Dataflow worker project to the same perimeter, or configure an ingress/egress rule to allow the specific access.
concept: VPC Service Controls, perimeter access, cross-project Dataflow, troubleshooting
